summary: Gruber defends the New York Yankees' league-leading payroll against critics who claim the team 'buys championships.' Drawing on Khoi Vinh's analysis, he argues that the Yankees actually reinvest a high percentage of their revenue (54%) into player talent, ranking fourth among MLB teams. He contrasts this with teams whose owners pocket revenue-sharing money rather than investing in winning. Gruber frames the criticism as incoherent—the same people mocked the Yankees for failing to win despite spending, then criticized them for winning because of spending. He argues the Steinbrenner family's commitment to winning is exactly what team ownership should look like.
key_points: Critics of the Yankees' payroll are incoherent: they mocked the team for not winning despite spending, then criticized them for winning because of spending
Relative to franchise value and revenue, the Yankees' 54% payroll-to-revenue ratio ranks only fourth in MLB, undermining the narrative of reckless spending
Many team owners pocket luxury tax revenue rather than reinvesting in talent, making the Yankees' spending look like responsible ownership by comparison
The Yankees were a last-place team when Steinbrenner bought them in 1973, so their success was built, not inherited
A team that 'spends unfairly' is simply defined as any team that spends more than your favorite team
money_quotes: A team that 'spends unfairly on payroll' is any team that spends more than your favorite team.
What exactly should the Yankees do with the profits the team generates, if not spend it on ballplayer salaries? Keep it in the Steinbrenner family pockets?
Yankees fans don't feel guilty about the Yankees payroll. It's not like the team is run at a loss. It's a business, and their business is winning baseball games.
Winning leads to profits from ticket sales, TV, and merchandise; profits are used to sign all-star-caliber talent; and the talent leads to winning.
The goal is not merely to be good, but to be the best, to win the World Series, every year, with no excuses when they fall short.
To try to be great, not hope to get lucky.
What I love about the Yankees and respect about the Steinbrenners is that they mean it, and act accordingly.
There was and never has been anything automatic about the Yankees' success. It has been earned season after season.
key_insight: Spending to win isn't unfair—it's what ownership is supposed to do, and the real scandal is teams that pocket revenue instead of investing in talent.
